key_insight: Catalyst discovery for ammonia synthesis is reframed as a multi-intermediate reaction-network problem, where useful materials must simultaneously satisfy compatibility across N*, NH*, NNH*, and HNNH* on a massive design space.
engineering_relevance: A ten-million-scale generative workflow can uncover rare catalyst families beyond conventional screening, including candidates tailored to dissociative or associative ammonia synthesis pathways.
method: Adsorbate-specific generative Transformers explored ~15 million configurations per intermediate.
Structural compression reduced the generated space before high-throughput machine-learning-potential evaluation.
Scale-dependent overlap analysis identified four-intermediate compatibility regions and DFT validated representative leads.
evidence_signal: The study reports that full four-intermediate compatibility appears only at ten-million-scale exploration, yielding 279 high-potential materials and DFT-backed examples such as Fe-V and Al-Pd-Zr.
watch_item: The approach depends on large-scale generative coverage and ML-potential fidelity, so missing chemistry or surrogate-model bias could hide viable catalysts.
paper_title: Reaction-Network-Level Discovery of Ammonia Synthesis Catalysts via Ten-Million-Scale Generative Exploration
